Module com.example.battleship
Package com.example.battleship.view

Class BattlefieldView3D

java.lang.Object
javafx.scene.Node
javafx.scene.SubScene
com.example.battleship.view.BattlefieldView3D
All Implemented Interfaces:
javafx.css.Styleable, javafx.event.EventTarget
public class BattlefieldView3D extends javafx.scene.SubScene
Full 3D battlefield: both boards (the player's and the machine's) exist in the same scene at all times, placed apart from each other, floating over one continuous "sea" slab, inside a box "sky" made of ordinary walls (not a SubScene fill gradient -- see createGradientTexture(javafx.scene.paint.Color, javafx.scene.paint.Color, int, int) for why). Only one board is actually in view at a time, because the OrbitCameraRig3D is always hovering over just one of them -- calling setTurn(com.example.battleship.view.BattlefieldView3D.Turn) glides the camera from one board's dome over to the other's.

  • Constructor Summary

    Constructors
    Constructor
    Description
    BattlefieldView3D(double width, double height)
    Builds both boards, the sea, the sky, the lights, and the camera rig (starting parked over the player's own board), all inside one SubScene.

  • Constructor Details

    • BattlefieldView3D

      public BattlefieldView3D(double width, double height)
      Builds both boards, the sea, the sky, the lights, and the camera rig (starting parked over the player's own board), all inside one SubScene.
      Parameters:
      width - initial width of the embedded SubScene
      height - initial height of the embedded SubScene

  • Method Details

    • buildWaterGrid

      private void buildWaterGrid(javafx.scene.Group boardGroup)

    • buildSeaFloor

      private void buildSeaFloor(javafx.scene.Group root3D, double boardSpan, double totalFieldDepth)
      One big flat slab under both boards, standing in for the open sea around them.

    • buildSkyWalls

      private void buildSkyWalls(javafx.scene.Group root3D, double boardSpan, double totalFieldDepth)
      Four tall wall panels around the sea, standing in for a sky. Uses a gradient baked into a texture image (via createGradientTexture(javafx.scene.paint.Color, javafx.scene.paint.Color, int, int)), applied as a normal diffuseMap -- the same well-tested code path as any other textured 3D shape. Deliberately does NOT use a LinearGradient as a live Paint anywhere in the 3D scene, since that's what caused the ghosting bug on SubScene.setFill().

      These are true flat planes (createWallQuad(double, double)), not Box shapes: a thin box has a front face and a back face only a few units apart, and viewed edge-on those two nearly-coincident surfaces z-fight, showing as flickering horizontal stripes. A single-sided plane has no second surface to fight with.

    • createWallQuad

      private javafx.scene.shape.MeshView createWallQuad(double width, double height)
      A single flat rectangle (2 triangles, no thickness), lying in its local XY plane.

    • createGradientTexture

      private javafx.scene.image.Image createGradientTexture(javafx.scene.paint.Color top, javafx.scene.paint.Color bottom, int width, int height)
      Bakes a vertical two-color gradient into a small bitmap once, up front -- a texture, not a live gradient Paint. This is the safe way to get a gradient look in this scene: apply it as a diffuseMap on ordinary 3D geometry, the same code path already confirmed stable for the sea floor and every ship, rather than feeding a LinearGradient into a Paint property that gets re-evaluated during rendering (which is what broke SubScene.setFill() when the camera moved).

    • setupLights

      private void setupLights(javafx.scene.Group root3D)

    • onMouseMoved

      private void onMouseMoved(javafx.scene.input.MouseEvent event)
      Shows a white ring on whichever player-board water cell the mouse is currently over, hides it otherwise.

    • addShipToPlayerBoard

      public void addShipToPlayerBoard(Ship3D ship)
      Adds a ship to the player's board (in the player board's own local 0..360 coordinates).

    • addShipToMachineBoard

      public void addShipToMachineBoard(Ship3D ship)
      Adds a ship to the machine's board (in the machine board's own local 0..360 coordinates).

    • addToPlayerBoard

      public void addToPlayerBoard(javafx.scene.Node node)
      Adds any other node (e.g. a shot mark) to the player's board.

    • addToMachineBoard

      public void addToMachineBoard(javafx.scene.Node node)
      Adds any other node (e.g. a shot mark) to the machine's board.

    • getPlayerBoardGroup

      public javafx.scene.Group getPlayerBoardGroup()
      Returns:
      the player board's own group -- controllers use this to tell, via node.getParent(), whether a picked node belongs to the player's board or the machine's

    • getMachineBoardGroup

      public javafx.scene.Group getMachineBoardGroup()
      Returns:
      the machine board's own group; see getPlayerBoardGroup()

    • setTurn

      public void setTurn(BattlefieldView3D.Turn turn)
      Glides the camera from whichever dome it's currently over to the other board's dome.

    • getCurrentTurn

      public BattlefieldView3D.Turn getCurrentTurn()
      Returns:
      whichever board the camera is currently parked over

    • getCameraRig

      public OrbitCameraRig3D getCameraRig()
      Returns:
      the camera rig itself, so a controller can wire up drag-to-orbit input once gameplay actually starts
